lesson.title: Binomial Distribution
lesson.body: The Binomial distribution models the number of successes in n independent Bernoulli trials, each with success probability p. If X ~ Binomial(n, p), the probability of exactly k successes is P(X=k) = C(n,k)·p^k·(1-p)^(n-k). The mean is E[X] = np and the variance is Var(X) = np(1-p).

rooms.question: What are the two parameters of the Binomial distribution?
rooms.explanation: Binomial(n, p) is parameterized by n (number of trials) and p (probability of success on each trial).

rooms.question: A fair coin is flipped 6 times. What is P(X = 4) where X counts heads?
For X ~ Binomial(20, 0.3), what is the variance?
rooms.explanation: Var(X) = np(1-p) = 20·0.3·0.7 = 4.2. The standard deviation is √4.2 ≈ 2.049.

rooms.question: Order the steps to compute P(X ≥ 1) for X ~ Binomial(5, 0.2):
rooms.explanation: Use the complement rule: P(X≥1) = 1 − P(X=0) = 1 − (0.8)^5 ≈ 0.672.

rooms.question: When does the Binomial distribution approximate a Normal distribution?
answers: When n is large and p is not too close to 0 or 1
rooms.explanation: By the CLT, Binomial(n,p) ≈ Normal(np, np(1-p)) when n is large and np ≥ 5 and n(1-p) ≥ 5.

boss.name: The Bernoulli Knight
boss.taunt: Each trial you face is independent. Fail one, and the others care nothing for your suffering.

questions.question: A manufacturing process has a 5% defect rate. In a batch of 100 items, what is E[number of defects]?
For X ~ Binomial(n, p), what happens to the distribution as p → 0 while λ = np is held constant?
questions.explanation: The Poisson distribution is the limit of Binomial(n,p) as n→∞, p→0 with np=λ fixed — the Poisson approximation.

questions.question: The PMF of Binomial(n, p) satisfies P(X=k)/P(X=k-1) = ?
questions.explanation: The recursive ratio is [(n-k+1)/k]·[p/(1-p)], which shows the PMF increases until k < (n+1)p and decreases after.
